Jeremiah receives a harsh commission: remind God's people of the ancient covenant made at Sinai, the promise bound by oath to Abraham and sealed by the exodus. But the reminder is not gentle exhortation—it opens with a curse. The LORD shows Jeremiah that this covenant, this very agreement that secured Israel's identity and inheritance, comes with teeth. Obedience leads to blessing; disobedience triggers judgment that is not abstract or distant but real and inescapable. Yet what breaks through these opening verses is not the curse itself but the recognition of betrayal. God had warned them "again and again" from Egypt to Jeremiah's own time. He did not spring judgment on them without witness. The curse was always there in the covenant, visible to anyone willing to see it, and they chose to ignore it anyway.

The chapter then reveals why this moment of proclamation is so urgent. A conspiracy runs through Jerusalem and Judah—not a plot of foreign invasion but something far more terrible. The people have returned to the sins of their ancestors, abandoning the covenant as though it meant nothing. They have multiplied their idols until there are as many altars to Baal as streets in the city, and they are even trying to silence the prophet who speaks God's words. What becomes clear is that this is not a nation in ignorance but a people in active rebellion, choosing false gods and then silencing the voice calling them back. God's response is unambiguous: judgment is coming, and when they cry out, He will not listen. The idols they have burned incense to will not save them. This is what covenant breaking looks like—not merely moral failure but the severing of relationship with the God who has kept His promises while they have broken theirs.

But there is something devastating happening to Jeremiah himself. He stands as the LORD's messenger in a house that has rejected the LORD's word. Those closest to him—the people of his hometown Anathoth—are plotting his death. They want to silence him, to "cut him off from the land of the living, that his name may be remembered no more." This prophet who faithfully carries God's warning is betrayed by his own people, treated as an enemy for speaking truth. His cry for vengeance in verse 20 is not the petulance of a wounded man but the desperate plea of one who has committed his cause to the righteous Judge. Jeremiah knows that his vindication rests not with popular approval or human protection but only with the God who "examines the heart and mind." The chapter closes with God's promise to punish those of Anathoth—but notice that Jeremiah himself is still standing, still serving, still speaking what he has been commanded to speak, even though the cost is his life.

This chapter confronts us with an uncomfortable reality: sometimes faithfulness to God means bearing rejection from those around us, and sometimes obedience requires us to proclaim hard truths that people do not want to hear. Jeremiah had no guarantee of vindication in his lifetime. He had only the LORD's word and the knowledge that his cause was committed to a righteous Judge. The covenant with all its promises and all its curses still stands. God keeps His word. The question each reader must answer is whether we will keep ours.
